full_text:
https://mozambique.healthcluster.org/ 1 
 
BULLETIN#04 
April 2026 
 
Humanitarian Context 
Mozambique continues to face a complex humanitarian crisis characterized by the overlapping impacts of climate-
related shocks and armed conflict, significantly affecting the health and well -being of vulnerable populations. In 
2026, floods in the central an d southern provinces resulted in population displacement, damage to health 
infrastructure, and an increased risk of waterborne disease outbreaks, disrupting the continuity of essential health 
services. 
At the same time, insecurity in Cabo Delgado continues to drive displacement and place additional strain on 
already fragile health systems. Humanitarian needs remain high, with priority given to the provision of primary 
health care, emergency services, men tal health support, and assistance to displaced populations and host 
communities. 
In this context, Health Cluster partners have maintained a coordinated response to ensure the delivery of essential 
health services, with a focus on integrated approaches including mobile clinics, outbreak response, sexual and 
reproductive health services, and water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) interventions. However, significant 
challenges persist, particularly in terms of access, resource availability, and the need to strengthen monitoring and 
reporting mechanisms. 
This bulletin provides an overview of the activities and achievements of Health Cluster partners during April 2026, 
highlighting collective efforts to respond to urgent needs and support the recovery of affected communities in the 
northern region (conflict) and southern region (floods). 
 
1. KEY FIGURES FOR THE HRP 2026 NORTHEN AREA (CABO DELGADO, NAMPULA 
AND NIASSA) 
Under the 2026 Humanitarian Response Plan, the Health Cluster will address three strategic objectives, monitored 
through 15 key indicators. These activities aim to reach 409,968 beneficiaries by the end of the year in 18 districts: 
15 districts in Cabo Delgado, 2 districts in Nampula province (Memba and Erati) and 1 district in Niassa province 
(Mecula).

HEALTH CLUSTER BULLETIN - MOZAMBIQUE
https://mozambique.healthcluster.org/ 2 
 
BULLETIN#04 
April 2026 
However, gaps in reporting persist for certain indicators, attributed in some instances to partner reporting 
challenges, and in others to a lack of adequate monitoring and reporting tools. The Health Cluster is actively 
working with partners to address these gaps and strengthen overall reporting and monitoring capacities. 
2. Health Cluster partners activities carried out in the Northen (conflict response) during 
the month (April_2026) 
Partner Activity 
Action Contre la Faim (ACF) 
 
 
 
 
 
During the current month ACF, carried out: 
Mobile brigade’s and clinics activities: 
• Helded in all three districts: Macomia, Meluco, and 
Quissanga. Mobile brigades were conducted in 
twenty (20) communities, specifically in Macomia 
(Songueia Centro, Ntapuala, Bangala -1, and 
Licangano) Meluco (Pedreira, Massasse, Mitambo, 
Nguia, Mariria, Pitolha, Citate, and Nsemuco), and 
Quissanga (Quissanga 2 and Milamba), 
• Mobile Clinics team carried out activities in 
Macomia, Quissanga and Meluco districts. In 
Macomia the mobile clinic was held in the 
communities of Bangala 2, Nanoa ressetlement. In 
Meluco district, the Mobile Clinics took place in the 
communities of Nangor oro, Imbada and Ncoripo. 
In Quissanga, the mobile clinic took place in 1° de 
Maio, Namange and Indique. 
Referral of critically ill patients from the 
community to provincial hospital in Pemba: 
• In the district of Meluco, 7 cases of critically ill 
patients were referred from the communities of 
Namagico (1), Nsemuco (2), Citate (2), and 
Mitambo (2) during the month of April. In Macomia, 
10 patients were referred, and in the district of 
Quissanga, 8 critically ill patients were referred to 
the Provincial Hospital in Pemba. 
 
International Organization for Migration (IOM) 
During the current month IOM, carried out: 
• Integrated Mobile Brigades in Chiure, Metuge, 
Memba, and Erati reaching host communities and 
resettlement sites providing a total of 8,226 health 
consultations. 
• Mental Health consultations were provided in 
Memba and Erati reaching 225 people. 
• Sexual Reproductive Health reaching 1901 
(Female 1109 and Male 792) young vulnerable 
people 1266, migrants 225 and sex workers 1890 
reached in Montepuez , 576 (Female 329 and Male 
247) young vulnerable people 474, migrants 39 
and sex workers 64, referred for health facilities in 
Montepuez, through sexual and reproductive 
health programming. 
 
Figure 5: Lecture on Malaria Prevention and the Importance of 
Vaccinating Children Under 2 Years of Age in Namange Community 
- Quissanga District, April 2026 
Figure 6: Patient care at the Sick Child Consultation (CCD) – 
Imbada Community, Meluco District, April 2026
https://mozambique.healthcluster.org/ 3 
 
BULLETIN#04 
April 2026 
 
 
World Health Organization (WHO) 
 
 
 
 
During the current month WHO, carried out: 
• Supported the National Institute for Health (INS) in 
implementing activities to investigate cholera -
related rumors in communities in the districts of 
Monapo, Nacala -Porto, and Mogovolas in 
Nampula Province by providing direct funding for 
vehicle logistics, meal allowances for community 
leaders, and stipends for technical staff. 
• Provided technical support to the district CTCs 
(Monapo, Nacala-Velha, and Mogovolas 
• Reviewed and supported the workflow within the 
CTC in Ilha de Mocambique district; 
• Assigned and provided on -the-job training to 
technicians on how to fill out individual patient 
forms; 
• Assisted in assessing needs and discussing 
solutions with provincial authorities; and preparing 
the chlorine solution. 
UNICEF 
 
During the current month UNICEF, carried out: 
• As response to internal displacements and host 
communities’ emerging health care needs, 
UNICEF deployed 58 integrated mobile brigades 
(IMBs) to provide life -saving primary health care 
services in the districts of Erati and Memba. 
• These brigades reached a total of 6,580 
beneficiaries, which included 3,387 children, of 
whom 1,852 were girls. 
• Furthermore, the services reached 2,653 women, 
including 476 pregnant women who received 
antenatal care services. 
 
 
 
Figure 7: Photos taken during training happening in Pemba for the 
Quissanga SDSMAS staff in April 2026 
Figure 8: Visit to the Cholera Treatment Centre (CTC) in the Ilha de 
Moçambique District 
 
Figure 9: Baivo/UNICEF/Erati District/ IMB in Mulovola Community
https://mozambique.healthcluster.org/ 4 
 
BULLETIN#04 
April 2026 
 
2. KEY FIGURES FOR ADDENDUM PLAN FOR FLOODS 2026 
For the 2026 humanitarian response plan addendum for floods, the health cluster will respond to three objectives 
and from these objectives, 15 indicators will be monitored reflecting activities that will cover by the end of the year 
a total of 538,725 beneficiaries from the total of people in need (619,627) in 18 districts in Gaza, Sofala, Maputo 
and Maputo City. In total more than 67,000 people have reached, of which 40,000 in Gaza. 
 
 
 
 
 
620K 539K $21,5 
People in need People to be 
target 
Financial 
requirement 
2.1 Indicators reported in April, 2026 
 
Health Cluster 5W matrix April 2026. 
 
In case no partner has reported on an indicator that indicator is not shown in the matrix. 
There is a lack of reporting for some indicators, some due to reporting issues from the partners, and some others 
lack adequate tools to report and monitor. As Health Cluster we are working with partners to improve reporting and 
monitoring. 
 
FLOODS RESPONSE IN GAZA, SOFALA, MAPUTO AND MAPUTO CITY
https://mozambique.healthcluster.org/ 5 
 
BULLETIN#04 
April 2026 
3. Health Cluster partners activities carried out during the month in response to the 
floods (April_2026) 
Partner Activity 
UNFPA 
 
 
 
In April, UNFPA supported emergency response across 
five flood -affected districts in Gaza: Xai -Xai, Guijá, 
Chókwè, Mabalane, and Mapai. 
 
Mobile brigades provide integrated sexual and 
reproductive health services to over 12,000 people, 
including family planning and antenatal care. Twelve SRH 
kits – enough to serve approximately 150,000 people – 
were distributed, containing essential medical -surgical 
supplies and m edicines for emergency -affected 
populations. 
 
In resettlement sites, UNFPA established three safe 
spaces: two for women and girls, and one for boys and 
young men. These spaces offer a safe and welcoming 
environment for sharing experiences, building skills, and 
accessing SRH information. Additionally, 1,000 dignity kits 
with hygiene and personal safety items were distributed to 
women and girls, supporting their protection, reducing 
exposure to gender-based violence, and improving access 
to essential services. 
These actions have significantly strengthened access to 
sexual and reproductive health services and GBV 
prevention and response within Gaza's humanitarian 
response. 
 
This bulletin is produced by the Mozambique Health Cluster. We acknowledge the support of all government 
agencies and health partners for their role in providing life-saving primary health services in the affected locations. 
Equally, we appreciate all the contributions of reports and information from field experiences while delivering the 
humanitarian response. 
To reference this bulletin: Mozambique Health Cluster Bulletin, #4/2026 
For more information, please contact: 
Dr. Emiliano Lucero - Health Cluster Coordinator | luceroe@who.int 
Health Cluster Team – healthclustermoz@who.int 
Useful links 
• ReliefWeb: https://mozambique.healthcluster.org

related.title: Yemen: (Government-Controlled Areas) IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot | March - December 2026 [EN/AR] (pdf)
related.summary: YEMEN (Government-Controlled Areas): IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot  March - December 2026 Overview One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue  experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with continued instability, localised  violence, security incidents, and a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis leaving millions  unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions  between March and May 2026. Of these, 1.4 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 4  (Emergency), indicating large food consumption gaps and that people are undertaking  extreme coping strategies, such as selling their last female animal, selling their house or land,  and begging.
related.excerpt: YEMEN (Government-Controlled Areas): IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot March - December 2026 Overview One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with continued instability, localised violence, security incidents, and a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026. Of these, 1.4 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency), indicating large food consumption gaps and that people are undertaking extreme coping strategies, such as selling their last female animal, selling their house or land, and begging. related.title: Yemen: (Government-Controlled Areas) IPC Acute Food Insecurity Snapshot | March - December 2026 [EN/AR]
related.summary: YEMEN: One in two people in Government of Yemen-controlled areas face high levels of acute food insecurity amid economic crisis and insecurity One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis, localised violence and civil unrest leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026, with 1.4 million people experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency). This is a marginal seasonal improvement in food availability and access compared to the previous period (September 2025–February 2026).
related.excerpt: YEMEN: One in two people in Government of Yemen-controlled areas face high levels of acute food insecurity amid economic crisis and insecurity One in two people in Government of Yemen (GoY)-controlled areas of Yemen continue experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity, with a deteriorating macroeconomic crisis, localised violence and civil unrest leaving millions unable to meet basic food needs. Nearly 5 million people are experiencing IPC Phase 3 or above (Crisis or worse) conditions between March and May 2026, with 1.4 million people experiencing IPC Phase 4 (Emergency). This is a marginal seasonal improvement in food availability and access compared to the previous period (September 2025–February 2026). However, these gains remain temporary and insufficient to offset persistent economic and structural pressures. The food security situation is expected to deteriorate during the J

related.title: The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy
related.summary: Global Energy Agenda May 19, 2026 Print this page The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy By Tatsuya Terazawa   Tatsuya Terazawa is the chairman and CEO of the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan. This essay is part of the  2026 Global Energy Agenda . The crisis triggered by the USｰIsraeli attack on Iran on February 28 has impacted the world in a serious manner.
related.excerpt: Global Energy Agenda May 19, 2026 Print this page The impacts of the Iranian crisis on Japan’s energy strategy By Tatsuya Terazawa Tatsuya Terazawa is the chairman and CEO of the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan. This essay is part of the 2026 Global Energy Agenda . The crisis triggered by the USｰIsraeli attack on Iran on February 28 has impacted the world in a serious manner. Japan is certainly not insulated from the crisis, especially in terms of the rising cost of energy. But despite its heavy dependence on imported energy, Japan has managed the crisis relatively well compared with most of its Asian neighbors. Five factors have helped Japan manage the crisis so far. The first is the high level of crude oil reserves. Japan had 254 days’ worth of reserves at the time of the attack. This level is much higher than those of other Asian countries, with the possible exception of China. T

related.title: Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update : Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti, April - September 2026 (pdf)
related.summary: Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti  Key Messages  This report provides an update to the February to September 2026  Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of  April 15, 2026. Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026  From April to September 2026, most of the country is  expected to remain in Crisis (IPC Phase 3).
related.excerpt: Gas prices and insecurity worsen food access in Haiti Key Messages This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of April 15, 2026. Haiti - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 From April to September 2026, most of the country is expected to remain in Crisis (IPC Phase 3). Certain communes in the Port-au-Prince Metropolitan Area (ZMPP), particularly Cité Soleil and Croix-des-Bouquets, are expected to face Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes, with internally displaced persons (IDPs) especially affected. Pockets of households in Emergency (IPC Phase 4) are also expected in Artibonite, Centre, and some communes in the Nord-Ouest. These households, highly exposed to one or more forms of violence, deteriorating purchasing power, or rainfall irregularities,

related.title: What Gulf states need in a US-Iran deal
related.summary: Bottom lines up front ABU DHABI—The talks that collapsed in Islamabad on April 12 were the first direct US-Iran conversation since 2015. After twenty-one hours, US Vice President JD Vance  announced  that there was no deal, that Iran had not accepted Washington’s terms, and that the naval blockade would be enforced. What he did not mention—and what apparently received too little attention during the marathon talks—was the six countries whose airports, energy infrastructure, and civilian neighborhoods had been struck by Iranian missiles and drones over the preceding forty days.
related.excerpt: Bottom lines up front ABU DHABI—The talks that collapsed in Islamabad on April 12 were the first direct US-Iran conversation since 2015. After twenty-one hours, US Vice President JD Vance announced that there was no deal, that Iran had not accepted Washington’s terms, and that the naval blockade would be enforced. What he did not mention—and what apparently received too little attention during the marathon talks—was the six countries whose airports, energy infrastructure, and civilian neighborhoods had been struck by Iranian missiles and drones over the preceding forty days. The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) states were not parties to the Islamabad talks, and they do not appear to have been formally consulted at the time. While the GCC has sought to draw attention to their shared vulnerabilities, including at an April 28 summit in Jeddah , they have been too often overlooked in this cri

related.title: Do aid flotillas make a difference for Palestinians under Israeli blockade?
related.summary: Israeli minister’s mistreatment of abducted activists sparks global condemnation. Flotillas carrying humanitarian aid to Gaza began in 2008, two years after Israel imposed a blockade on the Strip. Two boats succeeded, despite Israeli attempts to stop them.
related.excerpt: Israeli minister’s mistreatment of abducted activists sparks global condemnation. Flotillas carrying humanitarian aid to Gaza began in 2008, two years after Israel imposed a blockade on the Strip. Two boats succeeded, despite Israeli attempts to stop them. Since then, Israeli forces have forced several flotillas to turn back, and detained hundreds of activists, sometimes violently and in international waters. The latest attack is drawing global condemnation for the abuse of the activists, notably by a senior Israeli minister . Various countries have summoned their Israeli envoys in protest. But will they go further and take action against Israel’s dehumanising treatment of activists and Palestinians? Presenter: Tom McRae Guests: Ahmed Abo Askar – Engineering student who lobbies for sponsorship of orphans in Gaza. Dan Perry – Former Chief Editor of the Associated Press in Europe, the Midd

related.title: Inaugural Europe Gulf Forum Concludes in Greece, Advancing Long-Term Cooperation Between Europe and the Gulf
related.summary: Senior leaders from Europe and the Gulf met in Greece for three days of private discussions on geopolitics, economic resilience, energy, supply chains, defence, technology and investment, setting out next steps for continued cooperation. ATHENS, GREECE — May 18, 2026 —  The inaugural Europe Gulf Forum (EGF), hosted by Theodore Kyriakou, Chairman of Antenna Group, in partnership with the Atlantic Council, concluded today in Greece after three days of private, high-level discussions among prime ministers, heads of state, senior policy makers, and investors. Held from 15–17 May, the Forum brought together senior leaders from Europe and the Gulf at a defining moment of geopolitical fragmentation and rapid economic transformation, with a focus on strengthening long-term cooperation between the two regions across shared strategic priorities.
related.excerpt: Senior leaders from Europe and the Gulf met in Greece for three days of private discussions on geopolitics, economic resilience, energy, supply chains, defence, technology and investment, setting out next steps for continued cooperation. Held from 15–17 May, the Forum brought together senior leaders from Europe and the Gulf at a defining moment of geopolitical fragmentation and rapid economic transformation, with a focus on strengthening long-term cooperation between the two regions across shared strategic priorities. related.title: Mali: Food Security Outlook Update - Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center, April - September 2026 (pdf)
related.summary: Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center  Key Messages  This report provides an update to the February to September 2026  Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The  analysis is based on information available as of April 28, 2026. Mali - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026  Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes observed in the Ménaka  region will continue through September due to the severe  difficulties populations face in accessing food, linked to the  major deterioration of livelihoods and the high number of  displaced persons.
related.excerpt: Reduced food access worsening acute food insecurity in the north and center Key Messages This report provides an update to the February to September 2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. The analysis is based on information available as of April 28, 2026. Mali - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes observed in the Ménaka region will continue through September due to the severe difficulties populations face in accessing food, linked to the major deterioration of livelihoods and the high number of displaced persons. Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes will persist in Kidal through September 2026 due to degraded livelihoods.
